title: Ensure that a minimal audit policy is created

input_row.title: Ensure that a minimal audit policy is created
input_row.description: Kubernetes can audit the details of requests made to the API server. The --audit- policy-file flag must be set for this logging to be enabled.
input_row.rationale: Logging is an important detective control for all systems, to detect potential unauthorised access. Impact: Audit logs will be created on the master nodes, which will consume disk space. Care should be taken to avoid generating too large volumes of log information as this could impact the available of the cluster nodes.
input_row.audit: Run the following command on one of the cluster master nodes: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --audit-policy-file is set. Review the contents of the file specified and ensure that it contains a valid audit policy.
input_row.remediation: Create an audit policy file for your cluster. Default Value: Unless the --audit-policy-file flag is specified, no auditing will be carried out. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug-application-cluster/audit/
input_row.impact: Audit logs will be created on the master nodes, which will consume disk space. Care should be taken to avoid generating too large volumes of log information as this could impact the available of the cluster nodes.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The control requires verification that the kube-apiserver is started with the --audit-policy-file flag and that the referenced file contains a valid audit policy.
- The presence of the --audit-policy-file flag can be determined by inspecting the kube-apiserver manifest (if using static pods) or the process arguments on the host, but the contents of the referenced audit policy file are not exposed via the Kubernetes API or kubectl.
- Reviewing the actual contents of the audit policy file (to ensure it is "minimal" and valid) requires direct file access on the control plane node, which is outside the scope of Kubernetes APIs.
- Therefore, this check requires host-level access and subjective review of the file contents.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. SSH to a Kubernetes control plane node (master node).
2. Locate the kube-apiserver manifest file (commonly at /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml) or inspect the running process arguments using:  
   `ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver`
3. Verify that the --audit-policy-file flag is present in the kube-apiserver command line or manifest.
4. Note the path specified for the audit policy file (e.g., /etc/kubernetes/audit-policy.yaml).
5. Open and review the contents of the audit policy file at the specified path.
6. Confirm that the file contains a valid Kubernetes audit policy (refer to the official documentation: https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug-application-cluster/audit/).
7. Ensure the policy meets at least the minimal requirements for auditing as per organizational or CIS guidance.
8. Confirm compliance: The control passes if the --audit-policy-file flag is set and the referenced file contains a valid audit policy.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES BENCHMARK V1.11 PDF
